anchor.snippet: A mathematical proof is a deductive argument for a mathematical statement
A proposition that has not been proved but is believed to be true is known as a conjecture
A formal proof is a sequence of formulas in a formal language
In direct proof, the conclusion is established by logically combining the axioms, definitions, and earlier theorems

annotations.label: Sum of two even integers is even

anchor.snippet: Consider two even integers x and y
In proof by mathematical induction, a single "base case" is proved
Let N = {1, 2, 3, 4, ... } be the set of natural numbers

annotations.label: 2n − 1 is odd for all positive integers n

anchor.snippet: we can prove by induction that all positive integers of the form
Proof by contraposition infers the statement

annotations.label: If n² is even then n is even (contraposition)

anchor.snippet: contraposition can be used to establish that, given an integer
In proof by contradiction, also known by the Latin phrase reductio ad absurdum
Suppose that
Proof by construction, or proof by example, is the construction of a concrete example
Joseph Liouville , for instance, proved the existence of transcendental numbers
In proof by exhaustion, the conclusion is established by dividing it into a finite number of cases

annotations.label: Four color theorem proof by exhaustion

anchor.snippet: the first proof of the four color theorem was a proof by exhaustion with 1,936 cases
A closed chain inference shows that a collection of statements are pairwise equivalent
A probabilistic proof is one in which an example is shown to exist, with certainty, by using methods of probability theory
A combinatorial proof establishes the equivalence of different expressions by showing that they count the same object in different ways
A nonconstructive proof establishes that a mathematical object with a certain property exists

annotations.label: Irrational a, b with a^b rational

anchor.snippet: The following famous example of a nonconstructive proof shows that there exist two irrational numbers
A statement that is neither provable nor disprovable from a set of axioms is called undecidable
One example is the parallel postulate
Gödel's (first) incompleteness theorem shows that many axiom systems of mathematical interest will have undecidable statements
A particular way of organizing a proof using two parallel columns
